meta.title: Diagrammatic Text Stress — Every Diagram Slot, Filled With Prose The Slot Did Not Expect
meta.subject: the engine's text-fitting contract under adversarial input

scenes.kicker: TEXT STRESS // DIAGRAMMATIC // FIXTURE FOR THE FITTED TEXT RENDERING PASS — A SLOT-BY-SLOT TORTURE TEST OF EVERY DIAGRAM PRIMITIVE
scenes.title: A Torture Test of Every Diagrammatic Text Slot in the docent Engine — Long Labels, Long Subs, Long Cells, Long Captions
scenes.tagline: Every label, every cell, every axis tick, every callout — each filled with a sentence two hundred and fifty characters long, so the renderer's text-fitting contract has to do real work and the depth-review eye can confirm no slot truncates ugly or overruns its container.
scenes.footnote: This fixture exists so a human or a regression sweep can verify the long-text strategy works end-to-end: auto-shrink kicks in where the slot is small; multi-line wrap holds the line count where prose lives; the ellipsis fallback uses the real U+2026 glyph; and never, anywhere, does a mid-word clip or an off-frame overrun escape into the rendered video.

beats.narration: This fixture is the torture test — every diagram slot in the docent engine, filled with prose two hundred and fifty characters long, so the text-fitting strategy has to make a visible choice on every primitive.
Compared with the gallery films, this fixture trades narrative coherence for a clean diagnostic: each scene exercises one renderer at the edge.

scenes.kicker: TEXT STRESS // STRUCTURE — NODE LABELS, EDGE LABELS, AND SUB-LINES PUSHED PAST THEIR EXPECTED LENGTH
scenes.heading: structure scene — node labels, node sub-lines, and edge labels each carry prose long enough to force the renderer's fit logic to step down through every tier

nodes.label: A Distributed Coordination Service That Maintains Quorum Across Three Availability Zones With Eventually-Consistent Read Replicas In Two Additional Regions
nodes.sub: The control plane lives here; every write goes through a Raft leader and every read can be served from any quorum member, which makes the latency profile asymmetric and the failure modes interesting to draw on a single edge.

nodes.label: Edge Cache With An LRU Eviction Policy Tuned For The Long Tail Of Cold-Object Reads — Survives A Hot Origin Outage For Roughly Forty Minutes
nodes.sub: Reads it can serve cold from the LRU never reach the origin; reads it cannot serve are queued, dedup'd by request signature, and forwarded to the origin in a single batched fan-out per second.

nodes.label: The Aggregator That Joins Per-Tenant Activity Streams With The Region-Level Billing Ledger Inside A Single Logical Window Of Five Wall-Clock Seconds
nodes.sub: When a tenant straddles two windows the aggregator double-counts at most once; when it straddles three the aggregator drops the late event silently — the very corner the risk node calls out below.

edges.label: writes-through-after-quorum-commit ▸ replicated to edge cache asynchronously over the cross-region bus with at-least-once delivery and idempotent application
ships every committed tenant activity event downstream to the aggregator with a per-tenant watermark, sequenced by Raft log index, never reordered across that boundary

beats.narration: Three node labels and two edge labels are each over a hundred and twenty characters — long enough that the card's fitFont and the connector's fitFontSize both have to drop the font size while keeping the labels legible. We chose the auto-shrink strategy over truncation here so the reader can still read the whole label.
The aggregator is the hero — and the failure mode it owns is a load-bearing trade-off; we accepted late-event loss instead of holding writes open indefinitely.

scenes.kicker: TEXT STRESS // WALKTHROUGH — ACTOR LABELS WITH FULL DESCRIPTIVE NAMES AND MESSAGE LABELS THE LENGTH OF A COMMENT
scenes.heading: walkthrough scene — actor headers and message labels that stretch past their pill widths so the renderer has to wrap actors and shrink messages on the line

actors.label: Authenticated Developer With An Active Workspace Pinned To us-east-2c
actors.sub: the user the trace starts from — a real persona, not a stick figure

actors.label: Edge Gateway With TLS Termination, Rate Limiting, And A Per-IP Bucket
actors.sub: the choke point every external request crosses on its way into the platform

actors.label: Application Service That Owns The Tenant-Activity Domain And Streams Events Outward
actors.sub: the workhorse — every business-logic decision passes through here at least once per request

beats.narration: Each actor pill carries a long descriptive label and an even longer sub; the renderer wraps both inside the pill rather than letting them spill past the edge.

message.label: POST /v1/workspaces/{wid}/activity?include=billing&dedup=true&projection=full — issue a tenant-activity event with the full payload projection and idempotency hint

beats.narration: The forwarded request carries a deduplication hint and a projection hint; the gateway forwards it after rate-limit and auth, no transformation in between.

message.label: forward → activity-svc::ingest with the original idempotency key and the trace context attached; expect a synchronous 200 if the event was novel, 204 if it was a duplicate inside the dedup window

beats.narration: The application replies once it has committed the event to the Raft log; the response is short, but the request label was long, and that is the point of this case.

message.label: 200 OK — Raft log index 1839472; event accepted, will be aggregated within the next five-second window unless the watermark advances first

scenes.kicker: TEXT STRESS // COMPARE — COLUMN HEADERS WITH FULL VENDOR NAMES AND CELLS WITH THE FULL PROSE OF A DESIGN-DOC TRADE-OFF
scenes.heading: compare scene — long column headers, long criterion rows, and cells filled with multi-clause sentences instead of one-word verdicts

columns.label: Distributed Event-Sourced Commit Log With Per-Tenant Partitions
columns.sub: vendor option A — fully managed by the team that ships the runtime

columns.label: Single-Tenant Append-Only File With Synchronous Cross-Region Replication
columns.sub: vendor option B — the conservative choice; everyone's used it before

columns.label: Hybrid Tiered Storage With Per-Region Hot Tier And Global Cold Tier
columns.sub: vendor option C — newer; the operational story is less proven

rows.label: Operational complexity per consensus event measured during a typical week

cells.text: Five operators on call across three regions; one playbook per failure class; a typical week sees two playbook executions, mostly benign.
Two operators on call; the failure modes are well understood and well drilled; a typical week sees zero playbook executions and most months see fewer than five.
Unknown; this is a new system and the operational story is still being written, so the first six months would carry a real ops tax we cannot estimate.

rows.label: Time-to-first-meaningful-response under a regional partition that lasts thirty minutes

cells.text: Sub-second from the read replicas in the surviving region, but writes degrade to read-only until quorum is restored; a measured trade-off the team has lived with.
Reads continue but writes block until the synchronous replication catches up — the worst case is the full duration of the partition.
Hot tier serves reads from the local region; writes can be served from the cold tier but the latency triples and the user-visible effect is unhappy.

rows.label: Eventual consistency window across regional read replicas

cells.text: Bounded by the cross-region commit lag; ninety-ninth percentile around two seconds.
Synchronous replication guarantees zero drift; the cost is the write-latency picture above.
Eventual; the cold tier can lag the hot tier by minutes during a sustained cross-region traffic spike.

beats.narration: The cells in this scene are deliberately full sentences — most compare scenes use four-word verdicts. We chose this density over a clean two-word reading because the trade-off itself lives in the prose, not in the gloss.
The fit logic wraps to three lines and shrinks within the cell; nothing escapes the column. Without the controlled wrap the renderer would have dropped sentences off the side of the table.

scenes.kicker: TEXT STRESS // QUANTITIES — FIGURE CARDS WITH LONG LABELS AND VALUES THAT ARE NOT JUST INTEGERS
scenes.heading: quantities scene — figures with long uppercase captions, exponential-notation values, and full-sentence notes beneath the number

figures.label: Estimated maximum sustained throughput at the ninety-ninth percentile across all three regions
figures.note: Derived from a year of production traces; the headline number is an upper bound during peak black-friday-style traffic, not a typical week.

figures.label: Storage amortized per tenant per month under the current dedup-and-tier policy
figures.note: This is the median across the long tail; the heaviest tenant is over an order of magnitude larger and skews the mean without changing the median much.

figures.label: Operator pages per quarter under the current alert-routing rules
figures.note: Down from one hundred and seventy in the equivalent quarter last year; the on-call cost dropped roughly seventy-five percent.

beats.narration: Each figure carries a label two lines long, a value that is not a single digit, and a note that is a full sentence — none of the 250 slots are running at the size the original spec designed for, and the operator pages dropped 75 percent quarter-over-quarter.

scenes.kicker: TEXT STRESS // CHART — AXIS TITLES AND BAR LABELS THAT NEVER FIT AT THE BASE FONT SIZE
scenes.heading: chart scene — long axis titles and long bar-category labels exercising the SVG-text shrink-or-truncate path

xAxis.label: Quarter under measurement during the rolling year-long observation window across the production fleet

yAxis.label: Operator pages per quarter (events that woke a human up during off-hours)

data.label: Quarter one — before the alert-routing rewrite
Quarter two — during the rewrite rollout phase
Quarter three — first full post-rewrite quarter

beats.narration: Each bar's category label is a full descriptive sentence about that quarter — far longer than a single word. The shrink-and-truncate path keeps each label inside its slot rather than letting two adjacent labels overlap.

scenes.kicker: TEXT STRESS // TENSION — LEDGER CARDS WITH LONG LABELS AND LONGER SUB-LINES
scenes.heading: tension scene — chosen, rejected, and risk cards each filled with prose long enough to force the LedgerCard's fitFont to step down toward the floor

nodes.label: Centralizing the commit log inside a single Raft group across all regions
nodes.sub: default_max_turns.unwrap_or_default() — the commit log lives in one consensus group; every region defers to the leader for ordering, accepting the cross-region round-trip on every write.

nodes.label: Per-region commit logs reconciled by an eventually-consistent merge layer
nodes.sub: We rejected this in favour of strong global ordering — instead of a merge layer we pay the latency tax of cross-region consensus, which costs us a round trip per write at the worst geography.

nodes.label: A regional partition lasting longer than the consensus timeout permanently demotes the partitioned region
nodes.sub: The partitioned region serves stale reads until the partition heals and the operator runs the manual rebalance procedure — a non-trivial cost that the runbook does not yet automate.

beats.narration: We chose strong global ordering over per-region eventual consistency; the trade-off is the cross-region round trip on every write. That is the bill we pay for the consistency story above.
The residual risk is the partitioned-region case — the runbook covers it but does not automate it, and the on-call cost is real. We held this rather than fixing it because the failure rate is low.

scenes.kicker: TEXT STRESS // RECAP — BULLETS LONGER THAN ANY SLIDE-WARE EXPECTS
scenes.heading: recap scene — points that wrap to several lines, exercising the multi-line wrap and shrink-past-the-floor fallback
points: Every diagram slot in the docent engine has now been exercised at the upper end of its plausible character count — long labels, long subs, long cells, long captions — and the fit-then-wrap-then-ellipsis-with-U-plus-2026 strategy holds without any mid-word clip or off-frame overrun.

The trade-off the engine makes is uniform: it shrinks to a documented floor first, wraps to a bounded line count next, and only falls back to ellipsis when the floor cannot hold the text. This keeps the failure mode predictable so a depth-review eye can confirm it at a glance.

What still cannot be argued from this fixture alone is whether the readability floor is set correctly for every register — the playful register may want a higher floor than the grave one. That is the residual fragility we expect to revisit, not a regression.

beats.narration: Three long bullets — each wraps to several lines, then auto-shrinks if the line cap would be exceeded; never a mid-word cut. The renderer honours its contract here, but the readability floor is a limit we have not yet measured at scale.
